texts.label: text
texts.orig: CO 2 is the main greenhouse gas, and its concentration was raised from 200 ppm after the industrial revolution in 1840 to 419 ppm in 2022 ( Ahn et al., 2019 ). Recent investigations reveal that the global concentration of CO 2 has been exceeding every year and can reach 750 ppm by the end of the century ( Retallack and Conde, 2020 ; Tans, 2017 ). The main sources of its emissions are the power plants and industries that use fossil fuels as energy sources, which account for more than 60% of the total CO 2 emissions (37.5 gigatonnes of the 55.3 gigatonnes emitted CO 2 ), which also increase every year ( Fawzy et al., 2020 ). This growing CO 2 emission changes the natural balance and leads to catastrophic events such as sea-level rise, climate change, and global warming ( Horton et al., 2020 ; Pazani et al., 2022 ; Ritchie and Roser, 2020 ). Therefore, it is necessary to apply control and emission reduction mechanisms ( Wang and Zeng, 2019 ).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane technology scientists proposed hollow fiber membrane contactors (HFMCs) as an alternative to conventional CO 2 absorption-desorption columns due to their promising advantages and outstanding performance for CO 2 capture. However, the HFMC systems suffer from wetting phenomena; hence, the HFMC studies focus on optimizing the membrane material, liquid absorber, and operating conditions. As scientists have addressed the wetting phenomenon with these solutions, new issues have emerged. In addition, the feedback loop between the lab and the industrial scale has been ignored. In this review, the characteristics of different HFMC systems based on their membrane types, including porous, dense, and composite (e.g., thin-film composite and mixed matrix) membranes, are compared to clarify their advantages and disadvantages. Also, the scale-up and economic conditions were discussed in terms of HFMC's feasibility to highlight the importance of the lab and industry loop. Furthermore, the future direction is stated to accelerate the HFMCs' development and provide a clear strategy for achieving practical and theatrical CO 2 absorption through HFMCs.

On the other hand, membrane technology with promising performance and beneficial properties, such as low energy requirements, process simplicity, and low space requirements, has been considered by many researchers in recent years. Among the various materials, polymer-based membranes are the most widely used in industrial applications due to their low cost and easy processability. However, it faces challenges such as the permeability-selectivity trade-off and low capacity ( Ahmadi et al., 2018 ; Robeson, 1991 ). Hence, a hybrid absorption-membrane process, viz., MCs, is introduced as a charming system that connects the advantages of two single separation processes and covers the disadvantages ( Ahmed et al., 2020 ; Cui and deMontigny, 2013 ; Li and Chen, 2005 ; Rackley, 2017 ; Siagian et al., 2019 ; Warsinger et al., 2018 ; WitekKrowiak et al., 2012 ).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Up to now, various review articles on the application of MCs in CO 2 separation have been published ( Chang et al., 2021 ; Kim et al., 2021 ; Xu et al., 2019a ), but a limited number of them have addressed HFMCs. For instance, Li and Chen (2005) reviewed the effective factors on the efficiency and performance of CO 2 absorption using chemical solvents in HFMCs and also evaluated numerical models for the performance prediction of HFMCs. Mansourizadeh and Ismail (2009) reviewed acid gas absorption by HFMCs by focusing on using microporous membranes. Also, they discussed important aspects of MCs, including liquid absorbents, membrane characteristics, mass transfer, model development, and membrane modules. Ibrahim et al. (2018) focused on the wetting phenomenon in HFMCs by highlighting its main reasons, including the liquid absorbent, the pore size and porosity of membranes, and the reciprocal interaction of membranes and liquid absorbents, and presenting methods and approaches to prevent wetting. Vadillo et al. (2020) focused on the performance of the CO 2 desorption process and presented the main impact factors in HFMCs, including solvent and membrane material selection, membrane module design, mass transfer correlations, operational issues, modelling, and simulations. Ng et al. (2021) reviewed the modelling and simulation techniques for predicting gas separation performance in both laboratory analysis and industrial applications. Kammerer et al. (2023) focused on the various prospects for CO 2 capture in industrial plants that have been developed over the last 20 years. They investigated three main methods of CO 2 capturing in power plants including pre-combustion, post-combustion, and oxycombustion. Imtiaz et al. (2023) compared the MC system with conventional membrane technology for CO 2 separations and also they investigated the MC system based on the design criteria, operation conditions, challenges and solutions. Also, they provide a techno-economic analysis of MC for academic and industrial scale. Ramezani et al. (2022) analysed membrane material, membrane modification, membrane surface modification, shape, solvent characterization, operating parameters and costs, hybrid process, membrane lifetime, and energy consumption as well as commercially available systems of HFMCs in a limited manner. Furthermore, they investigated pore wetting as the main technical challenge in membrane contactor industrial implementation for postand pre-combustion CO 2 capture processes in detail. Some researchers focused on the modeling of HFCM in differnet systems and highlighted the requirement for more realistic modeling ( Rivero 2020 ; Zhang et al., 2014 ).

They investigated three main methods of CO 2 capturing in power plants including pre-combustion, post-combustion, and oxycombustion. Imtiaz et al. (2023) compared the MC system with conventional membrane technology for CO 2 separations and also they investigated the MC system based on the design criteria, operation conditions, challenges and solutions. Also, they provide a techno-economic analysis of MC for academic and industrial scale. Ramezani et al. (2022) analysed membrane material, membrane modification, membrane surface modification, shape, solvent characterization, operating parameters and costs, hybrid process, membrane lifetime, and energy consumption as well as commercially available systems of HFMCs in a limited manner. Furthermore, they investigated pore wetting as the main technical challenge in membrane contactor industrial implementation for postand pre-combustion CO 2 capture processes in detail. Some researchers focused on the modeling of HFCM in differnet systems and highlighted the requirement for more realistic modeling ( Rivero 2020 ; Zhang et al., 2014 ).

texts.orig: which is a set of long porous HFs inserted in a parallel configuration in a shell. One phase flows in tubes, and another phase flows on the shell side. Generally, the liquid absorbent flows through the lumen side (tubes), and gas flows through the shell. The fiber walls are made from membrane material, which provides indirect contact between two phases (without dispersion of one phase into the other) through media. Indeed, gas molecules (containing CO 2 molecules) transfer to the liquid absorbent (solvent) through membrane pores for chemical reaction and absorption ( Bazhenov et al., 2018 ; Cui and deMontigny, 2013 ; Feng et al., 2013 ; Feron, 2016 ; Qi and Cussler, 1985 ; Zhang and Wang, 2013 ; Zhao et al., 2016 ). In this configuration, the thin and large tubes with a radius lower than 2 mm and a length higher than 1000 mm are placed in a shell. The number of membrane tubes ranges from several to thousands. These tubes provide a high surface area, leading to a higher mass transfer rate; as a result, the HFMCs significantly have a higher density than other modules (7-50 times higher) ( Bazhenov et al., 2018 ; Rackley, 2017 ; Warsinger et al., 2018 ).

texts.orig: The driving force of the HFMC systems comes from the partial pressure of the components in the gas phase and the concentration in the liquid phase. The solvent determines the selectivity of the HFMCs, while the membrane material determines the selectivity of the conventional membrane separation (gas permeation) process. In the HFMC processes, CO 2 absorption efficiency from the gas stream can be achieved to 90% depending on the mass transfer and the membrane area, but the conventional membrane separation process is limited by the process conditions (especially the pressure difference between the lumen and shell sides) ( Mosadegh-Sedghi et al., 2013 ). Even reaching more than 95% or even 99% absorption by HFMCs is technically possible. Hence, due to its specific characteristics, the HFMC system can be proposed as the desirable MC type for CO 2 separation ( He, Q. et al., 2018 ; Sun et al., 2021 ).

texts.orig: 3.1. Wetting phenomenon
texts.text: 3.1. Wetting phenomenon

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The membrane is the most important part of the HFMC systems, which must have low water solubility or permeability and high CO 2 permeability to restrict water migration via the membrane and hinder the wetting of the membrane pores for efficient CO 2 capture. Hence, the wetting phenomenon is referred to as the main challenge of HFMCs with porous membranes, in which the liquid phase penetrates through the membrane pore and results in a pore blockage, higher mass resistance due to the low gas/liquid diffusion rate, and a significant decrease in the separation efficiency ( Chabanon et al., 2014 ; Gabelman and Hwang, 1999 ; Ibrahim et al., 2018 ; Ismail and Mansourizadeh, 2010 ; Keshavarz et al., 2008 ; Tofighy et al., 2020 ; Zhao et al., 2012 ).
texts.text: The membrane is the most important part of the HFMC systems, which must have low water solubility or permeability and high CO 2 permeability to restrict water migration via the membrane and hinder the wetting of the membrane pores for efficient CO 2 capture. Hence, the wetting phenomenon is referred to as the main challenge of HFMCs with porous membranes, in which the liquid phase penetrates through the membrane pore and results in a pore blockage, higher mass resistance due to the low gas/liquid diffusion rate, and a significant decrease in the separation efficiency ( Chabanon et al., 2014 ; Gabelman and Hwang, 1999 ; Ibrahim et al., 2018 ; Ismail and Mansourizadeh, 2010 ; Keshavarz et al., 2008 ; Tofighy et al., 2020 ; Zhao et al., 2012 ).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The state of membrane wetting in an HFMC system is divided into three categories: non-wetting, partial wetting, and wet membrane. The non-wetting state is an ideal condition (only considered in modelling studies), in which the membrane's pores are completely filled by gas molecules, and it leads to maximum mass transfer ( Fig. 1 (II)A) ( Xu et al., 2019b ). In the partially wetted membrane mode, which is a more realistic state, the liquid absorbent flows in pores, and the absorption occurs inside membrane pores ( Fig. 1 (II)B). The wetted membrane refers to the membrane pores filled with the liquid absorbent that is sometimes observed at the end of a long-term process ( Fig. 1 (II)C) ( Goh et al., 2019 ).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In HFMC systems, the liquid pressure must be higher than the gas pressure to evade membrane wetting. Also, the pressure difference between the two phases is essential for stable operation. The minimum pressure difference (breakthrough pressure) is determined based on liquid characteristics such as surface tension, contact angle, and membrane pore size ( Drioli et al., 2011b ; Franken et al., 1987 ; Ibrahim et al., 2018 ; Mosadegh-Sedghi et al., 2014 ).

texts.orig: 3.2. Liquid absorbent types in the HFMC systems
texts.text: 3.2. Liquid absorbent types in the HFMC systems

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The benefit of a suitable solvent could result in a high CO 2 absorption flux in an HFMC system with the same operational conditions. Hence, studying different liquid absorbents for CO 2 absorption is one of the important subjects in the HFMC systems. The critical properties of an ideal liquid absorbent for CO 2 separation are CO 2 solubility, reactivity, and reaction heat in the case of chemical absorption. Also, the breakthrough pressure, chemical and thermal stability, reservation energy, and thermal degradation of the liquid absorbent are important ( Song et al., 2018 ; Zhao et al., 2016 ).
texts.text: The benefit of a suitable solvent could result in a high CO 2 absorption flux in an HFMC system with the same operational conditions. Hence, studying different liquid absorbents for CO 2 absorption is one of the important subjects in the HFMC systems. The critical properties of an ideal liquid absorbent for CO 2 separation are CO 2 solubility, reactivity, and reaction heat in the case of chemical absorption. Also, the breakthrough pressure, chemical and thermal stability, reservation energy, and thermal degradation of the liquid absorbent are important ( Song et al., 2018 ; Zhao et al., 2016 ).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Liquid adsorbents are divided into physical and chemical adsorbents based on their gas absorption mechanisms. The selectivity of the liquid absorbent with physical-based absorption is provided by different gas solubilities in the solvent. Water and organic solutions are the most widely used liquid absorbents for physical CO 2 separation. On the other hand, the chemical-based absorption mechanism is based on the absorbent's reactivity with CO 2 molecules; various liquid absorbents such as amines ( Franco et al., 2012 ; Gong et al., 2006 ; Lv et al., 2012 ; Yang et al., 2013 ), amino acid salts ( Cao et al., 2019 ; Rahim et al., 2015 ; Zhang et al., 2009 ), water ( Belaissaoui and Favre, 2018 ; DashtArzhandi et al., 2015a ; Gomez-Coma et al., 2017 ), aqueous ammonia ( Makhloufi et al., 2014 ; Yu, 2018 ), alkaline solvents ( Chun and Lee, 1997 ; Dindore et al., 2004 ; Izaddoust and Keshavarz, 2017 ), ILs ( Dai et al., 2016a ; Rostami et al., 2018 ) have been used for CO 2 separation in the HFMC.

The selectivity of the liquid absorbent with physical-based absorption is provided by different gas solubilities in the solvent. Water and organic solutions are the most widely used liquid absorbents for physical CO 2 separation. On the other hand, the chemical-based absorption mechanism is based on the absorbent's reactivity with CO 2 molecules; various liquid absorbents such as amines ( Franco et al., 2012 ; Gong et al., 2006 ; Lv et al., 2012 ; Yang et al., 2013 ), amino acid salts ( Cao et al., 2019 ; Rahim et al., 2015 ; Zhang et al., 2009 ), water ( Belaissaoui and Favre, 2018 ; DashtArzhandi et al., 2015a ; Gomez-Coma et al., 2017 ), aqueous ammonia ( Makhloufi et al., 2014 ; Yu, 2018 ), alkaline solvents ( Chun and Lee, 1997 ; Dindore et al., 2004 ; Izaddoust and Keshavarz, 2017 ), ILs ( Dai et al., 2016a ; Rostami et al., 2018 ) have been used for CO 2 separation in the HFMC.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Among them, the amine-based absorbents are the most efficient and conventional liquid absorbents for CO 2 separation. MEA, as a primary amine, has a higher reaction rate, adequate capacity, and low cost among all amines. Due to that, aqueous MEA has been known as the most effective chemical solution for CO 2 absorption in HFMC or packed bed systems ( El Hadri et al., 2017 ). There are some drawbacks to amines as the most investigated solvent used in any carbon capture process. Amines are energy-intensive, have limited loading capacity, and most importantly, tend to degrade and corrode in atmospheric conditions. In addition, they are volatilization and contamination of the gas phase and regeneration costs ( Lu et al., 2015 ). The amino acid salts have high reactivity and compatibility with the environment and membrane material. Also, they have a reliable effect on the environment ( Rahim et al., 2015 ; Zhang et al., 2009 ). As another type of liquid absorbent, the alkali salts revealed high reaction activity with increasing the reaction temperature for the HFMCs ( Yan et al., 2007 ). However, membrane fouling and water loss from solvents are the main challenges in applying alkali salts ( Chun and Lee, 1997 ; Izaddoust and Keshavarz, 2017 ; Jin et al., 2017 ). The ILs are organic salts with a melting point at room temperature. The advantages of these solvents are their high CO 2 absorption capacity, low volatility, lower regeneration energy requirement, and adjustable properties ( Scholes, 2022a ). However, the properties and application of this material in real operational conditions need to be studied ( Dai et al., 2016b ; Golkhar et al., 2013 ; Rostami et al., 2018 ; Zunita et al., 2021 ). Therefore, it can be concluded that further investigation into introducing novel and acceptable solvents for the HFMC-based CO 2 separation processes is essential.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.3. Effect of operating conditions
texts.text: 3.3. Effect of operating conditions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The operation conditions including the gas mixture composition, flow configuration, and temperature and pressure of the system, are the three characteristics of any HFMC system that define system performance. In almost all lab-scale studies, the HFMC was used as an alternative system to the conventional CO 2 separation process, utilizing a gas stream including N 2 and CO 2 or pure CO 2 and a fresh liquid absorbent without any CO 2 loading at ambient temperature and atmospheric pressure. In a realistic condition, the gas mixture contains acid gas, the liquid absorbent has CO 2 loading, and the temperature and pressure are different due to the liquid absorbent regeneration and reaction. The liquid absorbent is not fresh and contains the CO 2 loading, which is regenerated in the desorption part of the HFMC unit. Besides these, the flow configuration (the geometry of the fibers, liquid flow in a tube or shell, and absorption/desorption design) as operation conditions should be carefully chosen to achieve the highest absorption rate with stable performance ( Kim et al., 2021 ; Qian et al., 2020 ; Vadillo et al., 2020 ).

texts.orig: limited to low CO 2 concentration and pressure processes, and the researcher must suggest an HFMC that can be used for high-concentration CO 2 gas streams with higher pressure, such as natural gas or syngas. Mansourizadeh et al. (2010) studied the effect of operation conditions (CO 2 concentration, flow configuration, and temperature) on the absorption flux with different liquid absorbent types. In the case of the chemical liquid absorbent, the CO 2 concentration influence was low; in contrast, it was significant for physical absorbents. Increasing the absorbent temperature led to higher CO 2 flux for chemical absorption, while the opposite results were observed for the physical absorbent due to a decrease in CO 2 solubility. In addition, partial wetting was observed for both absorbents for different reasons. As a result of various studies, it can be pointed out that operating conditions are very important to determine the cost-effectiveness of the HFMC process or the possibility of operating under the best operating conditions, and special attention should be paid to them.

texts.orig: 4. Membrane types and materials in the HFMCs
texts.text: 4. Membrane types and materials in the HFMCs

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The applied membranes in the HFMC systems can be categorized into three groups: microporous membranes (with pores diameters lower than 1 /u1D707 m) ( Fig. 1 (III)A), dense membranes ( Fig. 1 (III)B), and composite membranes ( Fig. 1 (III)C-D) ( Chabanon et al., 2014 ; Nguyen et al., 2011 ). The microporous membranes are made from commercially available polymeric materials such as PP ( Mohammaddoost et al., 2018 ; Nakhjiri et al., 2018 ; Yan et al., 2007 ), PE ( Belaissaoui and Favre, 2018 ; Rosli et al., 2019 ), PSf ( Kianfar et al., 2017 ), PVDF ( Abdolahi-Mansoorkhani and Seddighi, 2019 ; Ghasem et al., 2012a ; Zhang et al., 2008 ), and PTFE ( Cao et al., 2019 ; Ghobadi et al., 2018 ; Marzouk et al., 2010 ). The dense membranes ( Fig. 1 (III)B) eliminate the wetting phenomenon but have low mass transfer. The most common dense membrane material in HFMC systems is PDMS ( Rosli et al., 2019 ). The composite membrane is an alternative way to provide a membrane with long-term stability and high mass transfer ( Ahmadi et al., 2018 ). Researchers proposed the TFC membrane, comprising a thin film layer coated on the porous membrane as a support layer ( Fig. 1 (III)C) ( Lasseuguette et al., 2013 ). The presence of a thin layer can avoid liquid penetration and, eventually, the wetting phenomenon. The common membrane materials used as a top layer of TFC membrane are PDMS, PTMSP, PMP, TeflonTM AF, and PPO ( Villeneuve et al., 2018b ). The disadvantage of TFC membrane is the manufacturing problems, such as the distribution of pore shape and size in the support layer, material composition choice, high fabrication cost, and difficulty of commercial development.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In recent years, the dispersion of the inorganic/organic/hybrid fillers into the polymer matrix, known as the MMMs, has been proposed ( Fig. 1 (III)D). Various fillers have been used, including zeolites, metal oxides, carbon fillers, and MOFs ( Tofighy et al., 2020 ). The polymeric membranes filled with nanomaterials have a higher solubility than the neat polymeric membranes, and the overall mass transfer increment results from the polymer-filler interface ( Ansaloni and Deng, 2017 ; Ladewig and Al-Shaeli, 2017 ). Besides polymeric materials, ceramic materials have been considered a good alternative for CO 2 capture into an aggressive solvent or system with harsh operation conditions (high temperature or need of a chemically stable media). Indeed, ceramic HFMCs showed excellent mechanical and thermal stability compared to the polymeric HFMCs. In addition, their membrane's features as permeability and porosity can be changed by changing the sintering temperature ( Robeson, 1991 ). The disadvantages of ceramic HFMCs are their higher price and preparation difficulty, which result in lower growth compared to polymeric HFMCs ( Drioli et al., 2011a ).
texts.text: In recent years, the dispersion of the inorganic/organic/hybrid fillers into the polymer matrix, known as the MMMs, has been proposed ( Fig. 1 (III)D). Various fillers have been used, including zeolites, metal oxides, carbon fillers, and MOFs ( Tofighy et al., 2020 ). The polymeric membranes filled with nanomaterials have a higher solubility than the neat polymeric membranes, and the overall mass transfer increment results from the polymer-filler interface ( Ansaloni and Deng, 2017 ; Ladewig and Al-Shaeli, 2017 ). Besides polymeric materials, ceramic materials have been considered a good alternative for CO 2 capture into an aggressive solvent or system with harsh operation conditions (high temperature or need of a chemically stable media). Indeed, ceramic HFMCs showed excellent mechanical and thermal stability compared to the polymeric HFMCs. In addition, their membrane's features as permeability and porosity can be changed by changing the sintering temperature ( Robeson, 1991 ). The disadvantages of ceramic HFMCs are their higher price and preparation difficulty, which result in lower growth compared to polymeric HFMCs ( Drioli et al., 2011a ).

texts.orig: The porous polymeric membranes are the primary and most studied types of membranes used in the HFMCs for CO 2 separation, providing a wide range of porosity, contact angle, and cost. Therefore, researchers used different polymeric materials to determine the performance of the HFMCs with different liquid absorbents and operating conditions.

texts.orig: 4.1.1. Polypropylene
texts.text: 4.1.1. Polypropylene

texts.label: text
texts.orig: One of the most studied polymeric membranes for CO 2 absorption is PP, which is a highly hydrophobic membrane with other expected advantages of polymeric membranes, such as low cost, high void volumes, and good mechanical stability ( Bottino et al., 2015 ; Lv et al., 2010 ; Qi and Cussler, 1985 ). Researchers examined the performance of PP-based membranes with different solvents. For example, Al-Marzouqi et al. (2009) employed PP, super-phobic PP, and silicon rubber as the membrane fibers and NaOH and MEA solutions as the liquid solvents for CO 2 absorption from the CO 2 /CH 4 mixture by the HFMC. MEA had the highest CO 2 absorption efficiency compared to the NaOH solution at similar operation conditions, and the super-phobic PP has the best performance among other membranes.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Some researchers considered using the HFMC with an activated solution such as MEA, DEA, etc. to enhance the CO 2 absorption rate. For example, Lu et al. (2005) studied the performance of activated-MDEA with PZ (as an activator) for the PP fiber-based HFMCs compared to the non-active MDEA. Adjusting the operating parameters and using the activated MDEA results in higher CO 2 absorption of up to 99% and an absorption flux of 2.25 times higher than the non-active MDEA. Therefore, adding a low amount of activation material (PZ) can increase the overall mass transfer rate of the HFMCs compared to a similar system with a neat liquid absorbent.

texts.orig: Hence, others replace the conventional solvent with new liquid absorbents. Recently, amino acid salts gained much attention due to their desirable properties such as high surface tension, fast reaction rate with CO 2 , low volatility, and non-toxic behaviour. Niknam and Zare ( Niknam et al., 2020 ) studied the CO 2 separation in a commercial PPbased HFMC by amino acid salts (L-Proline promoted potassium carbonate solution). They improved the slow reaction rate of the used liquid absorbent by incorporating L-Proline (0.05 mol/lit), and the CO 2 recovery increased by 20 %. Yan et al. (2007) examined the PP-based HFMCs with potassium glycinate (PG), MEA, and MDEA and observed that only the PG solution showed stable separation in continuous separation for 40 h.

texts.orig: The water-based solution as a physical absorbent is one of the most studied new solutions. Hidalgo et al. (2020) reported that the separation performance of physical solutions, such as DI-water and sodium chloride, depends on the liquid absorbent flow rate, while for chemical absorbents such as NaOH, the gas flow rate has a significant influence on separation. This difference was related to the increment of reactive hydroxide ions in the liquid absorbent. In addition, they show that only NaOH can be used to increase the number of recirculation cycles without regeneration. In another work, Mohammaddoost et al. (2018) studied the new generation of solvents, viz., water-based NFs, in the HFMC systems with the PP membranes. Increasing the concentrations of the metal-oxide nanoparticles, i.e., SiO 2 , TiO 2 , and Al 2 O 3 , in the liquid absorbent had a positive influence on the mass transfer rate. The highest mass transfer rate was observed for Al 2 O 3 water-based solutions. In similar work, Peyravi et al. (2015) observed that the liquid flow rate and concentration of the nanoparticles (Al 2 O 3 , SiO 2 , CNT, and Fe 3 O 4 ) had significant effects on the CO 2 absorption, and the gas flow rate had a negligible effect on it.

texts.orig: On the other hand, some experts focus on CO 2 absorption at the labscale with a more realistic system. For example, Yang et al. (2013) reported that the existence of SO 2 in the CO 2 /CH 4 mixture resulted in a significant decrease in the absorption efficiency of the PP-based HFMC over a long time (30% by 15 days). Sohaib et al. (2020) conducted an attractive study about constructing a CO 2 separation unit based on the HFMC system. At pseudo-steady-state, CO 2 absorption with two ILs ([Emim][MS] and [Emim][DCA]) in the PP-based HFMC was compared in an absorption HFMC and the absorption/desorption HFMC units. The long-term stability test for 70 min showed the decrease of CO 2 absorption for both absorption HFMC and absorption/desorption, while the absorption efficiency reached 10% for absorption and stabilized 82% and 66% in absorption/desorption for [Emim][MS] and [Emim][DCA] ILs, respectively. Also, the mass transfer coefficients of the coupled process were 9 to 5 times higher than those of a single process. They proposed the combination of coupled absorption/desorption and ILs as an effective continuous post-combustion CO 2 capture process. However, the desorption part needs further improvement for long-term stability. In another work, Chavan et al. (2021) proposed a new strategy for enhancing the purity of the captured CO 2 with pH swing absorption in PP-based HFMCs. They injected enough acid and base components (1 M HCl and 1 M KOH) in the absorption and desorption stages to manipulate the acid-basic equilibrium. It decreased the CO 2 residual in the regenerated absorbent and improved the CO 2 degassing before desorption without adding any significant energy. Therefore, the maximum purity for the N 2 /CO 2 and H 2 /CO 2 mixtures was obtained at 99.97% and 99.96%, respectively.

texts.orig: Other strategies for improving the separation performance of PP membranes include using a coated dense layer on the PP surface and PP surface treatment. That's why, Yong et al. (2016) investigated the effect of layer coating (carbonic anhydrase) on the wetting phenomenon on the microporous PP and non-porous PDMS-based HFMCs for CO 2 absorption with K 2 CO 3 . They reported a significant reduction in pore blockage, even for hydrophilic membranes. Although the absorption rate of the coated PP membrane was higher than that of PDMS (70-90% higher), the long-term performance of the coated PP membrane showed pore wetting. Franco et al. (2012) used plasma to treat the membrane surface, which led to a higher CO 2 flux and a lower flux drop in the long-term separation process.

texts.orig: In summary, the CO 2 absorption efficiency is entirely high in most reports; however, the HFMCs with PP membranes have a low CO 2 absorption flux. The novel physical liquid absorbents (nanoparticle waterbased solutions and ILs) proposed superior long-term stability. Nevertheless, their absorption ability, cost, and dependency on operation conditions are still challenging, considering practical conditions. It should be mentioned that the main challenge in using different liquid absorbents for PP-based HFMCs is their long-term stability, which can be overcome using the dense layer. Also, in the case of nanoparticle absorbents, particles should be chosen based on their nanofluid stability and nanoparticle hydrodynamic diameter. The summary of the results of the studies for the PP-based HFMCs is presented in Table 1 . As seen, the highest flux belongs to the PP surface treatment. Although the PP surface treatment can significantly increase the membrane flux, future studies should address the overall cost of surface-treated PP when comparing the current materials.

texts.orig: 4.1.2. Polyvinylidene fluoride
texts.text: 4.1.2. Polyvinylidene fluoride

texts.label: text
texts.orig: PVDF is the well-known polymeric microporous membrane in the HFMC for CO 2 separation. It has been preferred over other polymers due to its solubility in a common solvent, which allows researchers to choose freely among different membrane preparation methods ( Mansourizadeh and Mousavian, 2013 ; Pang et al., 2018 ). Also, the separation performance of the PVDF membranes can be improved by adjusting their porous structure; in this order, the researcher used various additives. For instance, Pang et al. ( Pang et al., 2018 ) added LiCl, PA, and LiCl/PA into PVDF to investigate the mass transfer performance of the HFMCs for CO 2 separation. Incorporation of these led to changes in the membrane structure, as seen in Figs. 3 A and 5 B. They found that the contribution of the nodule-like zone was enhanced with the increasing non-solvent additive amount, and the portion of the porous area was reduced ( Figs. 3 C and 3 D). Fig. 3 E illustrates the CO 2 separation performance with different additives, in which the 8 wt.% PA-PVDF membrane had the highest mass transfer flux due to its advantageous structure.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Like other porous membranes, the PVDF membrane had a low wetting resistance. Hence, many studies enhance it with different additives. For example, Rongwong et al. ( Rongwong et al., 2009 ) investigated the effect of absorbent type (MEA, DEA, and AMP) on the membrane wetting of the PVDF in the HFMC system. The highest flux was reported for MEA and decreased to the lowest value among these liquid absorbents over time due to membrane wetting. The absorbent mixing did not decrease the wetting phenomenon. Thus, they added NaCl and SG to control the wetting phenomenon, and the MEA with SG showed no significant wetting and had stable CO 2 absorption flux.

texts.orig: In an interesting study, Gusnawan et al. ( Gusnawan et al., 2020 ) examined the CO 2 absorption mechanism of the soybean-based solvent containing 18 different amino acid salts via the PVDF-based HFMC. The solvent with K-arginate had a significantly higher CO 2 absorption flux than others, as indicated in Fig. 4 . On the other hand, the presence of K-glutamate and K-aspartate decreased the CO 2 absorption flux because they did not react with CO 2 . As shown in Fig. 4 B, The CO 2 absorption flux was mainly attributed to the hydrophilicity of the solvent. The introduction of hydrophilic amino acid salts in the solvent assisted the CO 2 absorption activities in the HFMC; on the contrary, hydrophobic amino acid salts inhibited them.

texts.orig: Kang et al. ( Kang et al., 2017 ) addressed the CO 2 absorption from a mixture with a high CO 2 concentration in a PVDF-based HFMC with MDEA solution. They also investigated the effect of operating pressure on outlet concentration, flux, absorption efficiency, and the overall mass transfer coefficient (K OG ) of CO 2 ( Fig. 5 ). The CO 2 absorption efficiency increased about three times (from 33% to 91%) by increasing the pressure from 1 to 60 bar for the gas mixture containing 70% CO 2 . Although the absorption efficiency increased, the limited mass transfer rate resulted in a lower CO 2 flux.

However, most studies considered a pure CO 2 gas stream to be the ideal condition. Another important point of available data for the PVDF-based HFMCs is the difference in CO 2 flux caused by the liquid absorbent. The highest CO 2 absorption flux for the PVDF-based HFMC is for distilled water and pure CO 2 , indicating the high potential of distilled water for this type of HFMC. Future investigations for this type of HFMC system should focus on the long-term stability of the CO 2 absorption compared to the separation flux for novel and conventional absorbents.

texts.orig: The PSf and PES are other well-applied polymeric materials in the HFMCs for CO 2 separation that have good hydrophobicity and acceptable thermal and mechanical stabilities. Also, it has been reported that PSf and PES can be used for spinning HF ( Ismail and Mansourizadeh, 2010 ; Rahbari-Sisakht et al., 2012 ). Several researchers improved the pore wettability and structure of the PSf membranes by incorporating an additive into the membrane dope solution. For example, Rahbari-Sisakht et al. ( Rahbari-Sisakht et al., 2012 ) compared the asymmetric PSf membranes containing different glycerols for CO 2 absorption. Fig. 6 displays the SEM image of hollow fiber PSf membranes. As seen, with increasing the glycerol content from 2 wt.% to 4 wt.%, large and thin finger-like holes increased at the contact surface. As a result, the CO 2 absorption exhibited an enhancement of the mass transfer rate by a factor of 1.5 in the HFMC with 4 wt.% glycerol compared to the HFMC with 2 wt.% glycerol. Similar results were seen in the study of Ismail and Mansourizadeh ( Ismail and Mansourizadeh, 2010 ).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.1.4. Polytetrafluoroethylene
texts.text: 4.1.4. Polytetrafluoroethylene

texts.label: text
texts.orig: PTFE has been suggested as the best porous membrane for HFMC systems in industrial applications due to its high wetting resistance, good chemical resistance, and commercial availability ( Bougie et al., 2014 ; Cao et al., 2019 ; Chabanon et al., 2011 ). The vital goal of any study on CO 2 separation with porous membranes in HFMCs is to suggest a membrane with better separation efficiency and lower membrane wetting. Thus, several studies compared PTFE-based membranes against the other above-mentioned porous HFMCs. For example, Li et al. (2018) modified the surface of PTFE HF by spraying silica nanoparticles on the membrane surface and used them for CO 2 /CH 4 separation by K 2 CO 3 aqueous solution. The best-fabricated membrane was a super-hydrophobic PTFE with a contact angle of 158.4° that rendered a high CO 2 absorption efficiency of about 97.1%.

The DEAPA showed higher capacity than the current amines, and parameters such as the flow rates of gas and liquid phases, DEAPA concentration, CO 2 partial pressure, and absorbent temperature had a positive effect on the process efficiency. texts.orig: As most studies consider an HFMC operation condition similar to room temperature and pressure, some researchers investigate the CO 2 separation from a high-pressure stream via the HFMCs. Marzouk et al. (2012) studied the absorption of H 2 S and CO 2 from a gas mixture at high pressure (up to 50 bar) by expanded PTFE and PFA membranes with aqueous NaOH, water, and amine solutions. The PFA had 10 times higher CO 2 flux than the PTFE, while at low pressure, the gas type had no considerable influence on CO 2 flux. In a similar study, Marzouk et al. (2010) revealed that the physical absorbents (aqueous NaOH) had better performance than chemical ones at high pressure.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.1.5. Other porous materials
texts.text: 4.1.5. Other porous materials

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The other porous membranes, such as PEI, PAI, PVC, and PEI-PA, are also used for CO 2 absorption in the HFMCs ( Ahmadi et al., 2017 ; Fashandi et al., 2016 ; Naim et al., 2018 ; Zhang et al., 2011 ). For example, Bakeri et al. (2015) compared the CO 2 absorption of the PEI-based and the PES-based HFMCs. The PEI casing solution was thermodynamically less stable, and the PES casting solution was less viscous (85% lower), which resulted in a more porous membrane structure. The mean pore size and effective surface porosity revealed the superior properties of the PES membrane. Therefore, the PES had a higher absorption flux (67% higher) at low liquid velocity due to its lower mass transfer resistance than the PEI. In addition, the researchers demonstrated that different combined materials could lead to higher CO 2 absorption in the HFMCs; for instance, the CO 2 absorption flux of the PEI-PA membrane is higher than that of PVC, PEI-PVDF, PAI, and PEI membranes.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.2. Dense membranes
texts.text: 4.2. Dense membranes

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Dense HFMs (self-standing) are also used for CO 2 separation in HFMCs due to their unique abilities, such as limiting gas losses, preventing membrane wetting, and having high mechanical resistance, making them suitable for difficult situations. A few reports exist about using it ( Belaissaoui and Favre, 2018 ). For example, Ansaloni et al. (2017) used several absorbents for CO 2 separation in the HFMCs, including a dense Teflon (AF 2400). The use of a dense layer could significantly decrease the absorbent losses (highly volatile solvents), which was an 80% decrease for the DEEA-MAPA blends and 100% for the MEA system. Therefore, it can be concluded that using a dense membrane can improve the prevention of absorbent loss, which is essential for an aqueous solution of amines and ammonia. While it probably decreases the flux, the absorbent can optimize the separation performance.

texts.orig: using conventional liquid absorbents, the application of novel liquid absorbents in the PVDF-based HFMCs is still in the initial stages. Another most studied porous membrane material is PTFE, which shows acceptable separation performance and is even suggested for industrial applications. The main disadvantages of the PTFE fiber for application in the HFMCs include the high cost and difficulty of small-diameter production. However, its separation flux for industrial applications should be studied in detail. On the other hand, the PP and PSf membranes have been considered in various research studies. Although the PP and PSf have low hydrophobicity, their CO 2 absorption flux can be improved by incorporating additives. Also, new solvents (ILs and water-based nanoparticle solvents) have been considered to overcome the conventional challenge. The absorption rate, cost, and operation dependency are still problems for these new solvents. Also, a few studies used dense HF for CO 2 separation in the HFMC, revealing that these materials could be the optimized choice for a process with a conventional liquid absorbent with high volatility, especially an aqueous solution.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As far as the studies go in the field of CO 2 separation with the HFMCs, a new challenge has emerged. The main problem was the limited performance of porous materials against the wetting phenomenon, which was not acceptable for industrial applications. Also, the predominant mechanism in dense membranes is the solution-diffusion theory, so they are subject to a performance constraint, viz., the permeabilityselectivity trade-off as evaluated by the well-known Robeson upper bounds ( Ansaloni and Deng, 2017 ). This limitation makes CO 2 separation membranes uneconomical in industrial applications.

texts.orig: Therefore, recent work has focused on introducing new membrane materials. They aimed to combine the advantages of organic and inorganic materials in the HFMC system. Finally, a new generation of more efficient membrane materials named composite membrane was introduced, which is known as the appropriate approach to solving significant challenges of the current polymeric membrane drawbacks, such as the trade-off relation between gas permeability-selectivity and wetting phenomena in dense and porous membranes, respectively. The preliminary results confirmed the composite HFMC as an alternative to previous membranes. Hence, the significant improvement in the performance of this membrane led to the rapid growth of studies that used composite membranes in the HFMC for CO 2 separation.

texts.orig: 5.1. Thin-film composite membranes
texts.text: 5.1. Thin-film composite membranes

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The TFC membranes are a composite membrane type with a coated layer of secondary material on the surface of a porous membrane layer, which aims to enhance the wetting resistance of the membranes and the packing density of the HFMCs ( Dai et al., 2019 ; Scholes et al., 2015 ). The coated layer can be made of polymeric or inorganic materials. If the coated layer was inorganic, these types of TFC membranes couple the chemical stability of inorganic material as a thin layer with the permeability of the polymeric support. In addition, coating a thin layer on the membrane-liquid interface changes the contact angle and porosity, which control the wetting phenomenon. The main characteristics of TFCs are their thin-layer characteristics, such as type, thickness (typically < 100 nm), and selectivity ( Dai et al., 2019 ).

The coated layer can be made of polymeric or inorganic materials. If the coated layer was inorganic, these types of TFC membranes couple the chemical stability of inorganic material as a thin layer with the permeability of the polymeric support. In addition, coating a thin layer on the membrane-liquid interface changes the contact angle and porosity, which control the wetting phenomenon. The main characteristics of TFCs are their thin-layer characteristics, such as type, thickness (typically < 100 nm), and selectivity ( Dai et al., 2019 ).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: So far, different dense layers have been coated on PP support for preparing TFCs and used in the HFMC systems for CO 2 absorption ( Makhloufi et al., 2014 ; Villeneuve et al., 2018a ; Villeneuve et al., 2018b ). Chabanon et al. (2011) used two different porous membranes (PP and PTFE) and two dense layers (Teflon and PMP coated on the PP) for CO 2 absorption with the MEA solution. They reported significant sensitivity of the PP membranes to the wetting phenomenon, good wetting resistance for the PTFE membranes, and better performance of both composite membranes. Dai et al. (2016a ) used six different membranes, including porous membranes (PVDF, PTFE, PP, and PBI) and two composite membranes (PMP and Teflon AF 2400-PP) for CO 2 absorption with [Bmim][TCM] IL. A higher CO 2 flux was reported for the PTFE, and higher long-term stability was observed for the Teflon-PP composite. Scholes et al. (2015) coated three thin, dense layers (PTMSP, PIM-1, and Teflon AF 1600) on the PP HFMs to investigate CO 2 absorption and compared their results with PP-based MCs. The highest CO 2 flux was observed for the HFMC with PTMSP and PIM-1 layers after 1 h of continuous absorption, and the water migration and wetting phenomenon was detected for the PP supports. On the other hand, the Teflon/PP HF had significant resistance against water and solvent sorption even after 10 h, resulting from the high hydrophobicity of Teflon.

texts.orig: Recently, TiO 2 and SiO 2 were used for fabricating PVDF-based TFCs in HFMC systems. For example, Xu et al. (2018) prepared a TiO 2 -SiO 2 /PVDF composite HFM for biogas upgrading applications. The HFMCs consisted of a mesoporous layer deposited onto the macroporous substrate. They investigated the CO 2 absorption with MEA (1 M), which observed good long-term stability (31 days) so that the CO 2 absorption flux was decreased by 10% up to 31 days because of the high contact angle and nano-dimensional pore size of the inorganic layer ( Fig. 8 ). Indeed, the high chemical resistance and hydrophobicity of added layer led to improvement in long-term separation performance. In other words, the added layer works as a protection against the corrosive MEA.

texts.orig: Table 5 summarizes the recent literature that applied TFC-based HFMCs for CO 2 absorption. Several researchers reported that the flux drop in the long-term process is lower than 15% ( Amirabedi et al., 2019 ; Chabanon et al., 2011 ; Xu et al., 2019b ). Also, most reports observed higher contact angles and porosity ( Ahmad et al., 2013 ; Ghodsi et al., 2015 ; Rahbari-Sisakht et al., 2012 ; Xu et al., 2019b ). Long-term stability and wetting resistance tests already show better performance of TFC membranes compared to porous membranes such as PP ( Xu et al., 2019b ). By comparing the recent reports on the TFC-based HFMCs, the fast growth of the coating layer from a simple inorganic layer to a complex structure can be seen. The critical common point of these recent studies is their emphasis on choosing the suitable material for the coated layer on the porous membrane supporter and providing an effective structure to control the wetting in the long-term (up to 20 days). Monitoring these two factors can significantly change the magnitude of the CO 2 absorption rate, and provide a stable separation (less than 10% flux reduction). Also, the addition percentage in a coated layer should be optimized based on absorption flux to control selectivity decrement ( Kamio et al., 2020 ; Xu et al., 2019b ).

texts.orig: 5.2. Facilitated transport membranes
texts.text: 5.2. Facilitated transport membranes

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The FTMs applied the reactive carriers to interact with the gas feed species. The reactive carriers are located in the backbone of the poly- meric chain, which leads to the additional reactive pathway for CO 2 transfer or so-called facilitated transport. The main benefit of FTMs is the simultaneous enhancement of the absorption process selectivity and the gas permeance ( Dai et al., 2019 ; Kamio et al., 2020 ). Indeed, the FTMs are the best way to overcome the permeability-selectivity tradeoff of the polymeric membranes. However, the synthesized FTMs were suffered from non-stability. The SLMs were the primary and promising carriers for CO 2 separation. Due to the non-stability problem, the FSCs as the polymeric membranes comprising CO 2 reactive functional groups (e.g., amino groups), were proposed. Although the non-stability problem was solved, the CO 2 permeance of FSCs was not high. The alternative solution was to use the polymeric matrix containing mobile carriers, including ILs, amino acid salts, and enzymes. Therefore, recent studies on the membranes focused on determining the CO 2 absorption performance by FTMs with carriers, such as amines, amino acid salts, carbonic anhydrase, and alkalines ( Kamio et al., 2020 ).

texts.orig: 5.3. Mixed matrix membrane
texts.text: 5.3. Mixed matrix membrane

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fabrication of the polymer matrix containing fillers, known as MMMs, is another approach to modifying the membrane structure and performance ( Talavari et al., 2020 ). These membranes contain dispersed particles as fillers in the bulk phase (polymer matrix). The MMM combines the advantages of polymeric materials, i.e., processability and low cost, with the superior separation performance of fillers ( Ahmadi et al., 2018 ; Rongwong et al., 2009 ). Fillers can be organic/inorganic/hybrid, spherical/tubular/sheet-like, hydrophilic/hydrophobic, porous/non-porous materials. The typical fillers are zeolites, CMSs, activated carbon, CNTs, metal oxides, silica, and lamellar nanomaterials, which are used in the MMMs for HFMCbased CO 2 separation.
texts.text: Fabrication of the polymer matrix containing fillers, known as MMMs, is another approach to modifying the membrane structure and performance ( Talavari et al., 2020 ). These membranes contain dispersed particles as fillers in the bulk phase (polymer matrix). The MMM combines the advantages of polymeric materials, i.e., processability and low cost, with the superior separation performance of fillers ( Ahmadi et al., 2018 ; Rongwong et al., 2009 ). Fillers can be organic/inorganic/hybrid, spherical/tubular/sheet-like, hydrophilic/hydrophobic, porous/non-porous materials. The typical fillers are zeolites, CMSs, activated carbon, CNTs, metal oxides, silica, and lamellar nanomaterials, which are used in the MMMs for HFMCbased CO 2 separation.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: MMT is a hydrophobic clay with a high aspect ratio consisting of a two-silicate layer with either an aluminium or magnesium hydroxide edge. This material enhances the hydrophobicity of membrane material even with low wetting resistance as an additive ( DashtArzhandi et al., 2015b ). For instance, Rezaei et al. (2014) fabricated MMT-filled PVDF MMMs for CO 2 separation by adding 1, 3, and 5 wt.% nano-fillers into the polymer matrix. The material selection and method resulted in a mixed matrix of HFs consisting of a sponge-like structure with a thin layer of skin at the surface, which led to remarkable wetting resistance. The long-term absorption of MMMs showed a stable flux after 14 days. The result of CO 2 absorption with water revealed a 50% increase in the CO 2 flux with a loading of 5 wt.% of MMT. In another study, DashtArzhandi et al. (2015b ) merged the MMT into the PEI membrane and studied the wetting resistance and membrane composite structure. The fabricated MMMs had a smaller pore size and higher hydrophobicity than neat PEI. The CO 2 flux was enhanced by incorporating 1 wt.% of the MMT in the polymeric matrix, whereas increasing the MMT loading from 1 wt.% to 3 wt.% led to a 25% decrease in the CO 2 flux.

texts.orig: ZSM has been used for different goals in the membrane technology field. However, their application in the HFMC systems has not been developed. This porous material has high hydrophobicity versus the lamellar structure of clay particles. Rahbari -Sisakht et al. (2017) and Rezaei-DashtArzhandi et al. (2016) used the ZSM-5 in PVDF HF for CO 2 separation in a liquid-gas contacting system. In the first study ( Rahbari -Sisakht et al., 2017 ), the fabricated MMM-based HFMCs exhibited higher surface roughness, porosity, and average pore size by increasing the ZSM-5 loading ( Fig. 10 A). They observed less than 20% initial flux decrease without further reduction until two weeks ( Fig. 10 B). In another study, Rezaei-DashtArzhandi et al. (2016) compared the MMMs of PVDF with higher content of ZSM-5 (up to 5 wt.%). The mass transfer resistance of the membrane and liquid side decreased by increasing the ZSM-5 filler loading in the membranes.

texts.orig: Rosli et al. (2020) dispersed three different types of modified silica nanoparticles in the PVDF matrix to enhance the CO 2 absorption flux by water in the HFMC system. The silica nanoparticles were chemically modified by PDMS, DMDCS, and HMDS. Then, the functionalized nanoparticles were added to the matrix to improve the characteristics and morphology of the PVDF membrane. The PVDF containing HMDSmodified nano-silica (TS-530) revealed the best filler dispersion with a porosity of 77.57%, selectivity of 22.5, and high thermal and mechanical stability. However, the PVDF/TS-530 could not reach the neat PVDF membrane CO 2 absorption flux as indicated in Fig. 11 .

texts.orig: CNTs are nano-scale cylinders made from graphene sheets with a high aspect ratio and exceptional strength and stiffness. The positive effect of CNTs as an additive on improving the performance of polymeric membranes has been reported. Talavari et al. (2020) loaded 5 wt.% of CNTs into the PVDF matrix and investigated the CO 2 absorption in their HFMCs. The contact angle of the prepared CNTs/PVDF MMM was increased by 25% and reached to 103°. Moreover, the CO 2 absorption of the MMM was increased two times compared to the unfilled PVDF, and the long-term stability test did not show any fouling and wetting phenomenon for MMM.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 6. The HFMC potential for the industrial CO 2 separation process
texts.text: 6. The HFMC potential for the industrial CO 2 separation process

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Various medium-sized industries industry plants consider different methods for reducing CO 2 emissions due to global warming, environmental safety, and increasing the CO 2 taxes every year (reaching to hundred Euros per ton of emitted CO 2 ). Also, global companies are trying to control CO 2 emissions, such as the European Union Emissions Trading System, with a goal of a 55% decrement in 2030 compared to the CO 2 emitted in 2005 ( Rivas et al., 2021 ; Chen et al., 2023 ). The membrane technology scientist proposed the HFMCs as a solution for this matter due to their flexibility and modularity. Compared to conventional separation units, the fundamental advantages of these units are their ease of implant, the same inflow as conventional separation towers, and low space requirement ( He et al., 2022 ). Fig. 12 displays a diagram of a pilot plant HFMC for CO 2 absorption from the gas stream. As can be seen, the flue gas enters the HFMC and contact with the lean liquid absorbent. The chemical reaction between the CO 2 and liquid absorbent results in CO 2 absorption from the gas mixture through membrane pores. Then, the rich absorbent pump to the HFMC contacts a sweep gas stream under low pressure or vacuum conditions ( Li et al., 2017 ; Nieminen et al., 2020 ; Scholes et al., 2020 ). One of the main concerns for this system is energy consumption. Although the regeneration of liquid absorbents under low pressure results in a higher energy penalty, avoiding solvent phase change reduces the overall energy requirement. In addition, controlling the solvent vaporization or using a solvent with low volatility can be effective ( Scholes, 2022b ). Thus, the energy consumption for the liquid absorbent regeneration including the energy consumption of the compressor and pump can make this process effective ( Chang et al., 2021 ).

The fast growth of studies in this field in recent years has been promising. Contrary to what is expected, there is a knowledge gap between the researchers' works and the industrial application of the HFMCs. Hence, the scale-up challenges and the economic aspect of the pilot-scale HFMCs are addressed in detail.

Researchers have since begun researching in this direction. texts.orig: The successful scale-up requires avoiding the use of ideal conditions for HFMCs. Therefore, a few recent researchers have considered an actual gas mixture and operational condition similar to the current industrial units for the scale-up of the CO 2 separation by the HFMC ( Li et al., 2013 ; Scholes, 2020 ). For instance, Scholes et al. (2014) reveal the difference between lab-scale studies and pilot plants for different MCs. They considered the PP and PTFE as the porous membranes and PDMS as non-porous membranes and reported the CO 2 absorption in HFMCs on the lab-scale and pilot-scale. In the lab-scale, the PP HF was thoroughly wetted, and the PTFE was wetted up to 69%. In contrast, both PP and PTFE were fully wetted in the pilot plant, showing the effect of operational conditions on the HFMC performance. It was due to pressure fluctuation, in which the pressure was greater than the breakthrough pressure. For the PDMS dense layer, they did not observe any membrane wetting, but the mass transfer coefficient was in the range of 10 -6 cm/s, while the mass transfer coefficient of PP and PTFE was in the order of 10 -3 cm/s. In another work, Scholes et al. (2020) used a PDMS composite HF to absorb the CO 2 from a flue gas similar to the outlet stream of coal-fired power with MEA 30 wt%. The HFMC was simultaneously used for CO 2 absorption and liquid absorbent regeneration. The trial presented a CO 2 recovery of over 90%, proving the HFMC viability for post-combustion CO 2 capture on an industrial scale. However, because of the thermal losses from uninsulated membrane modules and lack of energy integration, the pilot HFMC with composite membranes had an energy duty (approximately 4.2 MJ/kg of CO 2 captured) higher than the conventional CO 2 capture system. Indeed, A membrane system to capture CO 2 has roughly 10 -40% more energy than a plant of equivalent output without CCS and guarantees 80-90% reduced CO2 emissions.

texts.orig: Nieminen et al. (2020) studied a continuously operated CO 2 capture unit, including a PP-based HFMC for CO 2 absorption and a striper under vacuum conditions to regenerate the liquid absorbent (potassium glycinate solution) in two steps. First, a single HFMC was considered that its performance was enhanced with higher liquid flow rates, CO 2 concentration, and decrement by decreasing the desorption temperatures. In this step, the CO 2 absorption was restricted by low CO 2 desorption efficiency from the incorporated absorbent solution. In the next step, vacuum stripping was used, in which mass transfer coefficients were enhanced at higher temperatures (60 -80°C) and lower vacuum pressures (300 -800 mbar). This time, the desorption efficiency was still low due to mass transfer limitations in the stripper. They achieved high CO 2 purity with acceptable CO 2 absorption flux and indicated that the system bottleneck is the desorption unit which significantly increased the system heat duty requirement.

texts.orig: In addition to research focused on solving the current problem of the HFMC industrial unit, there has been some effort to change the HFMC configuration ( He and Zhang, 2020 ). He et al. (2018) proposed the crossflow HFMCs to improve industrial efficiency. The gas-phase flowed perpendicularly to the contactor fibers, while the liquid phase flowed into the tubes. The module ( Fig. 13 ) operated in a cross-flow pattern with the gas phase circulating in series and parallel and the liquid phase flowing in parallel, which led to easy maintenance, scalability, and production for the industrial-scale HFMC. However, the separation efficiency depended on the flow velocity, inlet flow shell geometry, and packing density, which determined the flow distribution.

texts.orig: The fast growth of simulation tools has resulted in reliable modelling of complex engineering units during the last decade. It has led researchers to prefer using these tools over laboratory testing for different separation systems such as the scale-up of HFMC for industrial applications ( Ng et al., 2021 ). Kimball et al. (2014) proposed a technoeconomic report on the performance of the HFMC with PTFE membranes for CO 2 absorption with an aqueous MEA solution. A 2D mass transfer model was used to predict a lab-scale HFMC separation system with reasonable precious and then used to investigate the pilot-scale feasi- bility of the HFMC. The 800 MWe (megawatt electrical) coal power station was considered, and the HFMC system requirements for pilotscale CO 2 separation were reported. The important result of this work was a significant difference in the HFMC volume in a different case. The four different operating conditions were considered, in which the module volume was three times higher in the worst case. They proposed that future studies study the uniform distribution of gas and liquid flows, module construction, and membrane material improvement. Usman et al. (2018) modelled the CO 2 capture with the HFMC using an [Bmim] [TCM] IL as the liquid absorbent. Based on modelling, the high efficiency (more than 90%) of the HFMC system, the effect of feed gas composition, and the liquid to gas flow rate (L/G) ratio were reported. Increasing the CO 2 content led to lower membrane area requirements with higher energy demands, and increasing the L/G ratio led to higher CO 2 capture efficiency with higher energy demands. The energy consumption was mainly related to the compressors (48% of total energy penalty), lean solvent pump (28%), and heat exchangers (25%). The estimation showed that the specific energy needed for this procedure is 0.75 MJ/kg CO 2 . Belaissaoui and Favre (2018) simulated the CO 2 capture by a dense HFMC in a novel absorption/desorption loop. The result showed a lower energy requirement for raw gas comparison and liquid absorbent pumping, which led to a lower total energy requirement (0.17 kWh/ Nm 3 raw gas). However, the methane loss of their novel loop was high (8.7%) compared to other works (lower than 1.3%).

texts.orig: The summary of the separation performance of the pilot-plant HFMC system is presented in Table 7 . As illustrated, most of these units are suffered from low CO 2 gas permeance. However, these works achieved high CO 2 absorption, whereas the long-term stability of the HFMC, especially with composite HFs, needs to be explored. The researchers reported the difference in membrane wetting behaviour at lab-scale and pre-pilotscale for CO 2 absorption ( Scholes et al., 2014 ). Indeed, the performance of the HFMCs-based CO 2 separation unit on a pilot-scale is very dependent on the liquid characteristic and operation condition in contrast to the lab-scale.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The main challenge in the practical unit is the engineering aspect (especially the desorption mood and loop configuration), the flue gas composition, and the feed gas pressure fluctuation. Also, the energy requirement is becoming an important factor while on a lab-scale, most reports consider a constant temperature due to using a lean solvent for all cycles. A few reported energy consumption for the scale-up HFMC shows that the energy penalty due to high heat loss and liquid absorbent regeneration increases the overall cost. Also, a significant difference in energy requirement was reported based on the type of liquid absorbent. Thus, the engineering aspect of the HFMC unit should be investigated (especially the energy consumption), and meanwhile, further studies should be focused on suggesting alternative membrane materials for preventing the wetting phenomenon should consider. Besides the engineering advantage of a pilot plan HFMC, the economic advantages are the real motivation for industrial application. The economic detail of reported studies in the HFMCs pilot plant is discussed in the next section.

texts.orig: The economic condition of the scale-up and application of the HFMCs is one of these aspects that has not been well studied, and reports mostly ignore it. Thus, few studies that reported the economic detail of the pilot-plant or industrial unit HFMCs are reviewed here to find a better prospect in this field. For example, Li et al. (2013) considered a conventional amine plant, in which the absorption column is replaced with the HFMC system, and the regeneration part remains (the hybrid HFMC unit). The total capital cost decreased by replacing the absorption column with an HFMC system. These decrements resulted from the possibility of using a liquid absorbent with lower reaction heat in the HFMC and were not limited to amine solvents for the HFMC. (2014) compared the HFMC separation process with the conventionally structured packing columns and pointed out that the cost per ton of CO 2 absorption for the conventional process is 62.4$, which was the range of the simulated HFMC (66-78 $). Therefore, the current primary HFMC in the pilot-scale indicated they could be an alternative process. These studies did not consider the HFMC for regeneration and focused on the HFMC application as an absorption unit. While, high-scale studies should consider a CO 2 capture unit, which the HFMC system uses for absorption and liquid absorbent regeneration simultaneously. In conclusion, the research in the pilot-plant of CO 2 separation with the HFMC reveals that the system needs to be optimized and more developed, mainly by the recently suggested liquid absorbents and membrane materials. Also, the emphasis on the HFMC system, as a replacement for conventional CO 2 separation units, is due to the lack of restrictions on the choice of liquid absorbent. However, there is a lot of work to do to provide a clear vision of the HFMCs-based CO 2 separation unit.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 7. Conclusion
texts.text: 7. Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In recent years, membrane contactor has been considered an alternative for gas separation. The HFMCs provided the highest surface area per volume and combined the advantages of the absorption method with the membrane technology. However, the wetting phenomenon significantly decreased the separation efficiency. Therefore, recent studies were conducted to improve the membrane performance by different approaches such as suggesting novel solvents and modifying the membrane. Different types of membranes (porous and non-porous) were used for CO 2 absorption with the HFMC systems, which have been reviewed in detail and compared based on the laboratory and pilot plant scales to provide a vision of both fields of challenges and potential.
texts.text: In recent years, membrane contactor has been considered an alternative for gas separation. The HFMCs provided the highest surface area per volume and combined the advantages of the absorption method with the membrane technology. However, the wetting phenomenon significantly decreased the separation efficiency. Therefore, recent studies were conducted to improve the membrane performance by different approaches such as suggesting novel solvents and modifying the membrane. Different types of membranes (porous and non-porous) were used for CO 2 absorption with the HFMC systems, which have been reviewed in detail and compared based on the laboratory and pilot plant scales to provide a vision of both fields of challenges and potential.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The PP and PSf membranes suffer from their low wetting resistance. However, their CO 2 absorption flux can be improved by incorporating additives or modifying the membrane surface. PVDF has high wetting resistance and ease of preparation, however, its performance under industrial operation conditions needs to be studied. The cost and low CO 2 absorption of PTFE in realistic operation conditions make it difficult to consider it as an alternative membrane. About dense membranes, only one study shows this type of membrane can optimize the highly volatile liquid absorbent, viz., aqueous amine and ammonia.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: On the other hand, composite membranes (TFC membranes, FTMs, and MMMs) were proposed as the potential solution for the current HFMC systems. Several studies reported higher CO 2 absorption by composite membranes compared to other materials; these membranes reveal alternative performance for CO 2 absorption in the HFMCs. The recently proposed FTMs have overcome the wetting resistance and trade-off between permeability and selectivity. On the other hand, there has been promising progress in MMM for CO 2 separation in the HFMC. The results do not show any CO 2 flux fall in long-term stability tests, and coating the different materials in the membrane matrix results in improving membrane specification and overcoming the past problem. It led to a new material generation for highly effective CO 2 separation in the HFMCs. In addition, various reports on the HFMC-based pilot plant were discussed in detail regarding the engineering and economic aspects.

texts.orig: The HFMC, as a hybrid absorption-membrane separation system, is considered an alternative design for carbon capture membrane contactors. The following suggestions are proposed based on the strengths and weaknesses of current literature in CO 2 separation with the HFMC:
texts.text: The HFMC, as a hybrid absorption-membrane separation system, is considered an alternative design for carbon capture membrane contactors. The following suggestions are proposed based on the strengths and weaknesses of current literature in CO 2 separation with the HFMC:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · In terms of long-term stability: The reports are only focusing on proposing novel membranes (MMM as an example), which to some extent does not consider the industrial limitations. The performance in industrial conditions regarding the long-term stability should be investigated.

texts.text: In terms of MMM-based HFMC: The superiority of MMM-based HFMC with novel nanofiller has been proved. Thus, further studies should be focused on using new fillers such as MOFs, ZIFs, COFs, MXene, GOs, and g-C 3 N 4 and combine past expertise to improve the porous membrane properties for achieving novel membranes.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · In terms of pilot plant and scale-up: The current studies show that the pilot plan requires optimization and development as well as alternative material. Indeed, the main barriers to HFMC implementation are engineering and economic aspects. Current industrial-scale HFMC is faced with the complete wetting of membranes. Thus, further works should be focused on both issues of industrial-scale HFMC as operation conditions (wetting phenomena, feed specification and flow dispersion) and the engineering aspects (energy consumption, economic and lifetime). Also, the performance of composite HFMCs in the pilot scale for both absorption and liquid regeneration parts should be reported.
